summary:
In patients affected by poliovirus infection resulting in motor neuron damage, particularly involving lower limb musculature such as those innervated by anterior divisions of lumbar spinal nerves L₂-L₄ where the quadriceps are located; weakened quadricep strength leads directly towards adopting an exaggerated flexion posture during ambulation characterized clinically as 'hand-to-knee walking'.
The primary cause for hand-to-knee gait observed post-polio can be attributed mainly to compromised functionality within the quadriceps group responsible for extension movements necessary throughout normal bipedal movement cycles. The resultant muscular imbalance forces compensatory strategies including increased reliance upon manual assistance while moving forward over short distances using hands placed firmly against knees instead of feet touching ground normally under healthy conditions without neurological impairment caused specifically through viral invasion targeting specific neural pathways crucial for voluntary control exertions required across multiple joints simultaneously when standing upright unsupported relying solely on skeletal structure integrity maintained via intact neuromuscular junctions functioning properly free from pathogenic interference disrupting homeostatic regulatory mechanisms essential maintaining overall physical well-being sustaining life activities requiring mobility capabilities fully realized unencumbered otherwise limited severely impacting quality daily living experiences profoundly altering individual autonomy levels considerably reducing independence The primary cause for hand-to-knee gait observed post-polio can be attributed mainly to compromised functionality within the quadriceps group responsible for extension movements necessary throughout normal bipedal movement cycles.
